metadata.summary: Configure UFW with nftables backend on Ubuntu 24.04: deny-by-default, SSH rate limiting, Docker port binding to 127.0.0.1, Cloudflare restrictions.

content.title: Firewall Management with UFW on Ubuntu 24.04
content.summary: Configure UFW (Uncomplicated Firewall) with nftables backend on Ubuntu 24.04 VPS: deny-by-default policies, service rules, SSH rate limiting, and Docker port binding to prevent UFW bypass. The critical rule: Docker publishes ports by binding to 0.0.0.0 which bypasses UFW — always bind Docker services to 127.0.0.1.

why.p: Every public VPS faces constant automated scanning. Without a firewall, every listening port is exposed. The most common misconfiguration is Docker silently bypassing UFW by injecting iptables/nftables rules directly — UFW cannot block what Docker publishes at 0.0.0.0.

list.item: Setting up a new VPS for the first time
Adding a new service that listens on a port
Auditing whether Docker services are accidentally exposed
Restricting web traffic to Cloudflare IPs only
Managed cloud environments with their own security groups (AWS SGs, GCP firewall rules) — use those instead of or in addition to UFW
When iptables is managed by another tool (docker rootless mode, k8s) — verify no conflicts before enabling UFW
Rate-limiting HTTP/HTTPS at the firewall level — use nginx limit_req for that; UFW rate limit is for SSH only

rule.statement: Set deny incoming, allow outgoing, and deny routed before adding any allow rules.
rule.rationale: Routed traffic must be explicitly denied or Docker VPN forwarding can pass through unfiltered. The order matters — defaults apply after user rules.

example.title: Set default UFW policies
example.code: sudo ufw default deny incoming sudo ufw default allow outgoing sudo ufw default deny routed # blocks forwarded packets (Docker/VPN)

rule.statement: Always add the SSH rule before running ufw enable.
rule.rationale: UFW applies rules immediately on enable. Missing SSH rule = instant lockout from the server.

example.title: Common UFW rules
example.code:
# SSH — always first, before enable sudo ufw limit 22022/tcp comment 'SSH'
# Web traffic sudo ufw allow 80/tcp comment 'HTTP' sudo ufw allow 443/tcp comment 'HTTPS'
# WireGuard sudo ufw allow 51820/udp comment 'WireGuard'
# Show rules sudo ufw status numbered sudo ufw status verbose

section.name: rate-limiting-ssh
section.p: ufw limit allows max 6 new connections per 30 seconds from a single IP. It cannot be tuned — for finer control use fail2ban.

example.title: Rate limit SSH with ufw limit

section.name: ufw-logging
section.p: Use low for production — logs blocked packets without flooding disk. Logs go to /var/log/ufw.log via rsyslog.

example.title: Enable and monitor UFW logging
example.code: sudo ufw logging low sudo tail -f /var/log/ufw.log sudo journalctl -k | grep "UFW BLOCK"

section.name: why-docker-bypasses-ufw
section.p: Docker inserts its own chains (DOCKER, DOCKER-USER) into nftables/iptables at a higher priority than UFW's rules. UFW rules live in the INPUT chain but never see Docker's published ports — they hit DOCKER-FORWARD first.

rule.statement: Never publish Docker ports to 0.0.0.0 on a production server. Always bind to 127.0.0.1.
rule.rationale: Binding to 0.0.0.0 publishes the port to all interfaces, bypassing UFW entirely. 127.0.0.1 restricts access to the host loopback — only processes on the same machine can connect.

example.title: Docker Compose binding to 127.0.0.1
example.code: # docker-compose.yml services: postgres: ports: - "127.0.0.1:5432:5432" # localhost only redis: ports: - "127.0.0.1:6379:6379" # localhost only rabbitmq: ports: - "127.0.0.1:5672:5672" # AMQP localhost only - "127.0.0.1:15672:15672" # Management UI localhost only

example.title: Exposed Docker port bypassing UFW
example.code: services: postgres: ports: - "5432:5432" # Expands to 0.0.0.0:5432 — bypasses UFW!

section.name: verify-docker-exposure
section.p: After any Docker Compose change, verify no services accidentally bind to 0.0.0.0.

example.title: Check for exposed Docker ports
example.code:
# Show all listening ports with binding address sudo ss -tlnp
# Show Docker port mappings docker ps --format "table {{.Names}}\t{{.Ports}}"
# Check for exposed 0.0.0.0 ports (should return nothing except 80/443) sudo ss -tlnp | grep '0.0.0.0' | grep -v ':80\|:443\|:22'

section.name: ssh-tunnel-for-internal-services
section.p: Access services bound to 127.0.0.1 from your local machine via SSH tunnel — no need to expose ports.

example.title: SSH tunnel to internal service
example.code:
# Forward local 5432 to server's 127.0.0.1:5432 ssh -L 5432:127.0.0.1:5432 nero@server-ip -N -f
# Connect locally psql -h 127.0.0.1 -p 5432 -U nero

section.name: production-examples
section.p: NERO production server UFW rules, multi-domain setup, debugging an exposed Docker port, and emergency lockout recovery via Hetzner Cloud Console.

example.title: NERO production UFW rules
example.code:
Status: active Logging: on (low) Default: deny (incoming), allow (outgoing), deny (routed)
To Action From -- ------ ---- 2222/tcp ALLOW IN Anywhere 80/tcp ALLOW IN Anywhere 443/tcp ALLOW IN Anywhere
example.note: No per-service rules. All backend ports (8100, 8101, 5432, 6379) bind to 127.0.0.1 and are accessed via nginx or SSH tunnel.

example.title: Debugging exposed RabbitMQ port
example.code: # Before fix — docker-proxy listening on 0.0.0.0 $ sudo ss -tlnp | grep 15672 LISTEN 0 4096 0.0.0.0:15672 0.0.0.0:* users:(("docker-proxy",pid=1234))

example.title: Fix exposed port in docker-compose.yml
example.code: # Fix in docker-compose.yml services: rabbitmq: ports: - "127.0.0.1:15672:15672" # was "15672:15672"

example.title: After fix verification
example.code: # After fix — bound to 127.0.0.1 $ sudo ss -tlnp | grep 15672 LISTEN 0 4096 127.0.0.1:15672 0.0.0.0:* users:(("docker-proxy",pid=5678))

example.title: Multi-domain setup via nginx
example.code: nero.faion.net → CF → nginx:443 → 127.0.0.1:8100/8101 meetingtax.io → CF → nginx:443 → 127.0.0.1:8000/3000 eulaguard.com → CF → nginx:443 → 127.0.0.1:8001

example.title: Emergency lockout recovery via Hetzner console
example.code: # In Hetzner console VNC session sudo ufw disable sudo ufw allow 22022/tcp sudo ufw enable sudo ufw status

template.purpose: Standard web server + Docker host initial UFW setup script
Restrict HTTP/HTTPS to Cloudflare IPs only
Docker Compose with all services bound to 127.0.0.1
Monthly cron script to refresh Cloudflare IP ranges in UFW

script.purpose: Status report: rules, listening ports, Docker mappings, exposed 0.0.0.0 ports, recent blocks
